title: Block Organ Transplant Purchases from China Act of 2025
short_title: Block Organ Transplant Purchases from China Act of 2025
plain_summary: This bill stops Medicare, Medicaid, and private insurance companies from paying for organ transplants that happen in China or use organs that do not come from the official US organ system. The new rule starts in January 2026. Doctors and hospitals that do these banned transplants can face criminal charges and have to pay big fines that cost up to three times more than the transplant procedure. The bill is meant to make sure organ transplants follow proper rules and come from approved sources.
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Energy and Commerce, and in addition to the Committee on Ways and Means, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such

sections.title: Section 1 Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Block Organ Transplant Purchases from China Act of two thousand twenty five or the Block Act of two thousand twenty five.

sections.title: Section 2 Prohibiting Certain Organ Transplants
sections.text:
Starting January first, two thousand twenty six, Medicare will not pay for certain organ transplants that have connections to China. These are called prohibited organ transplants. Medicare also will not pay for any medical care related to these transplants, like follow up visits, lab tests, or medicines. The only exception is if the medical care is needed to save someone's life after they already got one of these prohibited transplants.

Medicaid also will not pay for prohibited organ transplants starting January first, two thousand twenty six. Like Medicare, Medicaid will not pay for any related medical care either, except when it is needed to save a life after someone already got a prohibited transplant.

Private health insurance plans also cannot cover prohibited organ transplants or any related medical care starting with plan years that begin on or after January first, two thousand twenty six. The same exception applies for life saving care after someone already got a prohibited transplant.

Starting January first, two thousand twenty six, doctors and hospitals cannot provide prohibited organ transplants or any related medical care except to save someone's life after they already got a prohibited transplant. If a doctor or hospital breaks this rule on purpose, they can be fined, sent to prison for up to two years, or both. The government can also make them pay a civil penalty equal to three times what it cost to provide the prohibited transplant or care.

A prohibited organ transplant means transplanting a human organ that either happened in China or uses an organ that was not obtained through the official Organ Procurement and Transplantation Network in the United States. The organs covered include kidneys, livers, hearts, lungs, pancreases, bone marrow, corneas, eyes, bones, intestines, and other body parts that can be transplanted.

By January first, two thousand twenty six, the Secretary of Health and Human Services must write rules to explain exactly which medical items and services are prohibited and how to calculate the cost for civil penalties.
